_meta.dataPolicy: WSZYSTKIE dane SYNTETYCZNE (przyklad.test, NotkaApp/SklepDemo/PipeDemo, identyfikatory testowe, PESEL placeholder 00000000000). ZERO realnych danych klienta/PII.
_meta.note: Pytania modułu M3. Metadane z master allocation (rozkład globalny). Plik <800 LOC (AGENTS).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant selects a temperature range for a task requiring formality and repeatability.
questions.prompt: You need to generate test case steps for documentation in NotkaApp — the result should be formal and as repeatable as possible when run again. Which temperature range is most appropriate?

options.text: Low, about 0.0-0.3
Medium, about 0.5-0.7
High, about 0.8-1.2
Maximum, about 1.3-1.5

questions.feedbackCorrect: A low temperature (~0.0-0.3) produces conservative, more repeatable responses, which is exactly what test documentation requires.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The higher the temperature, the greater the randomness and the lower the repeatability — the opposite of what we want in documentation. Review screen M3.2 on the temperature scale.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant identifies the main risk of a high temperature in QA tasks.
questions.prompt: A team runs the same prompt about SklepDemo test cases twice at temperature 1.1. Which risk to QA work is most characteristic here?

options.text: Lack of repeatability — two runs produce divergent steps, which hampers review and regression
The response always gets cut off mid-sentence
Responses become shorter and more concise than at a low temperature
The result becomes identical on every run

questions.feedbackCorrect: High randomness causes content drift and a lack of repeatability between runs, which undermines the reliability of documentation and regression.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Cut-off output is caused by max tokens, and repeatability decreases (not increases) as temperature rises. Review screen M3.3 on the risk of high randomness in QA.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant recognizes the symptoms of a response cut off by the max tokens limit.
questions.prompt: After generating a list of test cases, the response ends with an unfinished sentence and an unclosed list, with no summary. What most likely happened?

options.text: The response was cut off after reaching the max tokens limit
The temperature was set too low
top-p narrowed the vocabulary of QA terms
The model deemed the task non-compliant with policy

questions.feedbackCorrect: An unfinished sentence, an unclosed list, and the absence of a summary are typical symptoms of exceeding the max tokens limit.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Temperature and top-p control randomness, not length — it is max tokens that hard-limits the number of response tokens. Review screen M3.5 on recognizing cut-off output.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant matches the randomness setting to the nature of the task (creative vs. formal).
questions.prompt: A tester has two tasks: (1) brainstorming unusual test data for a form in SklepDemo, and (2) writing the final test case steps for documentation. How should the temperature reasonably be set for each of them?

options.text: Task 1 — higher temperature; task 2 — low temperature
Task 1 — low temperature; task 2 — higher temperature
Both tasks — maximum temperature for a richer result
Both tasks — temperature 0.0, because that is always the safest

questions.feedbackCorrect: Brainstorming benefits from diversity (higher temperature), while documentation requires repeatability and formality (low temperature).
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The choice is reversed: a creative task benefits from higher randomness, and a formal one from low randomness. Review screen M3.2 on mapping temperature to task type.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant links a generation parameter setting to its effect on the formality, diversity, and length of the output.
questions.prompt: Match the generation parameter setting to its effect on the output.

pairs.right: A more repeatable and formal response
A more diverse and creative response
A response cut off before the end
A narrower, more stable selection of subsequent tokens

questions.feedbackCorrect: Temperature controls formality/diversity, max tokens controls length, and top-p controls the breadth of the pool of candidates for the next token.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The roles got mixed up: randomness (temperature, top-p) is not the same as length (max tokens). Review screens M3.2 and M3.4 on the generation controls.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant links a generation parameter setting to its risk or use in QA work.
questions.prompt: Match the generation parameter setting to its consequence in the context of QA test documentation.

pairs.right: Steps diverging between two runs of the same prompt
Missing final steps or acceptance criteria in the response
A stable, safe vocabulary of QA terms

questions.feedbackCorrect: High randomness risks a lack of repeatability, too low a max tokens loses the end of the document, and top-p regulates how broad and risky the word selection is.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Note that it is randomness (temperature/top-p) that is responsible for content risk, and max tokens for the missing ending. Review screens M3.3 and M3.4.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant compares two generation presets and chooses the safer one for test documentation.
questions.prompt:
You need to write the test case steps for logging into NotkaApp as persistent documentation. There are two presets to choose from for the same task:
Variant A: temperature 0.2 / top-p 0.3 / high max tokens.
Variant B: temperature 1.0 / top-p 0.95 / low max tokens.
Which choice is safer and why?

options.text: Variant A — low randomness gives repeatability and formality, and high max tokens protects against cutting off the document
Variant B — high temperature and top-p will give a richer, more detailed description of the steps
Variant B — low max tokens will force conciseness, which is always better in documentation
Either one — generation parameters do not affect test documentation

questions.feedbackCorrect: For documentation, repeatability and completeness matter: low randomness (A) limits drift, and a margin of max tokens protects against the steps being cut off.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: High randomness threatens repeatability, and low max tokens risks cutting off the document — these are not advantages in documentation. Review screen M3.6 on choosing the safer preset.
